messages.content: What kind of substrate should I use in an indoor sulcata tortoise enclosure?

The substrate for an indoor sulcata tortoise enclosure should be a mix of organic material and inorganic material. Good options for organics are coconut coir, organic topsoil, and peat moss. For inorganics, you can use sand or limestone. The substrate mix should be about 50% organic and 50% inorganic, with a depth of at least 6 inches to allow your sulcata tortoise to dig and burrow. It should also be kept moist but not waterlogged to prevent respiratory problems.
What is the best way to set up a sulcata tortoise indoor enclosure

Here are some general guidelines for setting up a sulcata tortoise indoor enclosure:

1. Size: Sulcata tortoises can grow large, so the enclosure should be spacious enough to accommodate their needs. At least a 4-foot by 8-foot area is recommended for a single tortoise.

2. Lighting: Lighting is crucial for tortoises as it helps them regulate their metabolism and produce vitamin D3. You will need to provide UVB lighting for 10-12 hours a day and a basking light for warmth.

3. Heat: Sulcata tortoises require a basking area with a temperature of 100-110°F and a cooler area with a temperature of 70-80°F. You can achieve this by using heat lamps or ceramic heaters.

4. Humidity: Sulcata tortoises need higher levels of humidity, and their enclosure should be kept moist but not soaking wet.

5. Substrate: As described earlier, use a substrate mix of organic and inorganic materials with a depth of at least 6 inches to allow for burrowing.

6. Water: Provide a shallow water dish for your tortoise to drink and soak in. Change the water frequently to prevent bacterial growth.

7. Diet: Sulcata tortoises are primarily herbivores, so their diet should consist of grasses, weeds, and hay. Supplement with calcium and vitamin D3.

Remember to research in more detail the specific needs of your tortoise breed and provide them with appropriate care.

My roommate, Bryan, used to be my boyfriend but we broke up as a mutual decision and decided to try to remain friends. He has a 4 year old daughter who stays at our house on the weekends and overall it has become apparent that he will do nothing but play video games and make a mess of the house when he is not at work. His dog and daughter cause me to clean up the house a lot more than I would normally, and he is always bragging about how he makes over $100 k a year with his new job. This week I expressed to him that I would really appreciate it if he would do the dishes because they were almost entirely dirty dishes that he had left in the sink from feeding his kid over the weekend but it was already 4 days past her departure and the sink was full of the gross dishes and flies were building up in the mess. He said he would do it after he finished playing his video game for the night but did not end up doing it because he fell asleep. He also has not swept the floor or vacuumed at all in the last month and I do it nearly every day even tho the mess on the floor is almost always from his dogs shedding fur and dirt from being outside. We both agreed that going forward we can date other people and it wouldn’t be an issue, but I had a date in my room the other night without telling him and he walked into my room to get some socks that he had in there since he hasn’t fully moved his stuff out of the room yet and he became furious that there was someone in the room without his knowledge and while his kid was there. I can understand his frustration because he told me he wanted me to let him know before anyone came over and not to have anyone there while his kid is there and also the person in my room is my x boyfriend who I am trying to work things out with and he was not that great of a guy when we broke up and Bryan witnessed him acting a bit eccentric due to his hurt feelings over the break up which we have now reconciled but he probably thinks my ex could be a threat to his daughter which isn’t true at all but I guess is a fair line of reasoning. He became so angry that he left the house and said very mean and hurtful things to me but after thinking on it I feel bad for not telling him that someone was coming over when I said I would do that but I honestly don’t think I should have to tell him when someone is coming over. If we are platonic roommates then that info is a curtesy but not required in our own private paid rooms away from common and shared parts of the house. Also I can’t really plan to just never have friends or dates over every single weekend because that just isn’t fair when I work during the week and so do most people so it doesn’t seem fair to tell me that I can’t ever do anything at the house with guests every day of every weekend simply because he has his daughter there with him so long as my guests and I are respectful of their time and space and don’t make an issue. I also have a huge problem with the bills being paid on time and have expressed so many times that I expect them to be paid on time so I don’t have to cover for him and cause my financial situation to be at risk. The utility bill and the electric bill both come out in the middle of the month and are due upon receipt each month. He has lived here for 3 months so this is not a new process to him. I asked him a few days ago for the electric bill and had to ask a few times if he could send it after not receiving any of the times I asked. I expressed that I am short on money this month and need him to go ahead and send it over. He finally sent it and then the utility bill came and I asked for him to send me the money so I could get it paid, he didn’t acknowledge my text that said this and then I ended up paying the full thing myself and then asked in person if he could send it, reiterating that I’m short on money this month. He said he would send it over asap. The next day he still had not sent it so I asked about it once again and he said he was short on money this month and asked if he could pay it at the end of the month when rent is due and he has another pay check. I reminded him I am short on money and really need him to pay it now but he says “I was not aware that the bills came out in the middle of the month. I am short on money idk what to tell you don’t you have thousands in savings? Im barely skirting by and already gave you money for the electric that I didn’t have” so I said “not sure where you got the idea I have thousands in savings since I don’t even have a savings account but fine pay me at the end of the month.” He didn’t say thank you and seemed so rude in the way he was asking and trying to suggest that if I had a savings that gave some excuse for not having the money for the things that he contributes to the cost of  and also this has been a reoccurring bill on the same date each month not a surprise and he always brags about how much money he makes which is quite a bit more than I do so I’m not sure where all his money is going. I also bought us a washer this month since the one at the house broke. Can you tell me what the best course of action is to address my issues with the situation and also not seem like I’m just nagging a ton but expressing my deep concern with how I am being treated and how he is not helping out with the house but expecting to control me and my life

It sounds like there are several issues going on here, including communication, expectations, boundaries, and financial responsibility. Here are some possible steps you can take:

1. Have a calm and honest conversation with Bryan about your concerns. It might be helpful to make a list of specific issues you want to address before the conversation, so you can stay on track.

2. Be clear about your expectations regarding cleaning, bills, and guests. Explain that you expect him to contribute equally and be responsible for his share of the household duties and bills.

3. Set some boundaries regarding guests and privacy. Explain that you will let him know when you have guests over, but remind him that you have the right to have guests in your private space.

4. Create a system for bill payment that works for both of you. Consider creating a shared account to make it easier to keep track of expenses and avoid late fees.

5. Consider creating a chore chart or schedule to make sure that household duties are divided fairly and evenly. This can help ensure that everyone is contributing equally.

6. If things do not improve after the conversation, consider seeking the help of a neutral third party, such as a mediator or counselor. A neutral third party can help you both communicate more effectively and find solutions that work for both of you.

Remember, effective communication and compromise are key to creating a happy and healthy living environment.
Does Bryan have a right to tell me that I am not allowed to have someone in the house if they are respectful and in my private space?

As long as the guest is not interfering with Bryan's right to privacy or causing any harm, it is not fair for him to dictate who can and cannot enter your private space. However, it is important to consider that his child is also present in the shared living space, and it is reasonable to consider the child's safety and comfort when having guests over. Therefore, it may be helpful to communicate with Bryan before inviting guests over, especially when his child is present or his schedule is affected. Ultimately, effective communication and respect for each other's rights and boundaries are key to maintaining a positive living environment for everyone involved.

Can you help me design a chore chart that evenly divides sweeping, mopping, vacuuming, taking out the trash, washing windows and blinds, dusting fans and surfaces, wiping down the countertops, cleaning the bathrooms, tidying up common areas, and doing yard work based on how often they need to be completed each week and how much each person should do to ensure each of us is doing an equal amount and not having to clean more than 30 mins a day

Certainly, here's a sample chore chart that you can customize to fit your needs:

**Chore Chart**

|        | Sunday | Monday | Tuesday | Wednesday | Thursday | Friday | Saturday |
|--------|--------|--------|---------|-----------|----------|--------|----------|
| Sweep  | Bryan  | You    |         |           |          | Bryan  | You      |
| Mop    | You    | Bryan  |         |           |          | You    | Bryan    |
| Vacuum | Bryan  | You    |         |           |          | Bryan  | You      |
| Trash  | You    | Bryan  |         |           |          | You    | Bryan    |
| Windows| Bryan  | You    |         |           |          | Bryan  | You      |
| Dusting| You    | Bryan  |         |           |          | You    | Bryan    |
| Countertop| Bryan| You  |         |           |          | Bryan  | You      |
| Bathroom| You   | Bryan  |         |           |          | You    | Bryan    |
| Tidy up| Bryan  | You    |         |           |          | Bryan  | You      |
| Yard work| You | Bryan  |         |           |          | You   | Bryan    |

This chore chart assigns one person to each task per day and alternates between the two roommates. Each task occurs twice a week. This means that each person will do approximately the same amount of work every week. You can adjust the days to suit your schedules.

Also, you can set a time limit of 30 minutes per day for each task and adjust the chart accordingly. It's important to communicate and make sure that both of you agree to the chore chart and the division of tasks.
Can you redo the chore chart to add in laundry and dishes as well. And I’m a bit confused on how the chart is laid out can you try to make it show clearly what day each chore is assigned to who?

Certainly! Here's an updated chore chart that includes laundry and dishes, and it has been reformatted for clarity:

|        | Sunday | Monday | Tuesday | Wednesday | Thursday | Friday   | Saturday |
|--------|--------|--------|---------|-----------|----------|----------|----------|
| Sweep  | Bryan  | You    | Bryan   | You       | Bryan    | You      | You      |
| Mop    | You    | Bryan  | You     | Bryan     | You      | Bryan    | Bryan    |
| Vacuum | Bryan  | You    | Bryan   | You       | Bryan    | You      | You      |
| Trash  | You    | Bryan  | You     | Bryan     | You      | Bryan    | Bryan    |
| Windows| Bryan  | You    | Bryan   | You       | Bryan    | You      | You      |
| Dusting| You    | Bryan  | You     | Bryan     | You      | Bryan    | Bryan    |
| Countertops| Bryan| You | Bryan  | You       | Bryan    | You      | You      |
| Bathroom| You   | Bryan  | You     | Bryan     | You      | Bryan    | Bryan    |
| Tidy up| Bryan  | You    | Bryan   | You       | Bryan    | You      | You      |
| Yard work| You | Bryan  | You     | Bryan     | You      | Bryan    | Bryan    |
| Laundry| You  | Bryan  | Bryan   | You       | You      | Bryan    | Bryan    |
| Dishes | Bryan | You   | You     | Bryan     | Bryan    | You      | You      |

This chore chart follows the same format as before and adds laundry and dishes to the mix. The chart is split into days of the week, with each column representing a different day. The person assigned to a particular chore is listed next to the chore on the assigned day. For example, on Sunday, Bryan is assigned to sweep, and you are assigned to do the laundry.

Again, feel free to customize the chart to fit your specific cleaning needs and schedules. And remember, communication is key to ensure that both of you are comfortable with the division of labor.

what is a list of things that should be done daily, weekly, or monthly (and how often for each item) in order to keep a clean 3 bedroom house that maintains an order and cleanliness and ensures that only about 30 mins every day needs to be devoted to cleaning. This is just in general and not based on the chore chart you created previously

Here's a general list of tasks that can help maintain a clean and organized 3-bedroom house:

**Daily Cleaning**

- Make beds
- Wipe down kitchen counters and stove
- Wash dishes or load them into the dishwasher
- Sweep or vacuum high-traffic areas
- Tidy up living spaces when needed
- Wipe down bathroom surfaces and fixtures

**Weekly Cleaning**

- Clean bathrooms thoroughly (toilets, showers, sinks)
- Dust all surfaces
- Mop or vacuum all floors
- Change bed linens
- Clean kitchen (sink, appliances, cabinets)
- Clean mirrors and windows
- Take out the garbage and recycling
- Do laundry

**Monthly Cleaning**

- Vacuum or wipe down furniture
- Clean the oven, dishwasher, and refrigerator
- Dust blinds and baseboards
- Wash curtains
- Deep clean carpets
- Clean air vents and fans

It's important to note that the frequency of cleaning tasks may vary depending on your specific needs and lifestyle. For example, if you have pets or children, you may need to clean floors and furniture more frequently. However, by dividing and incorporating these tasks into your daily routine, you can ensure that your home stays clean, organized, and easy to maintain with as little as 30 minutes of cleaning per day.

Based on the list of cleaning items can you now create a chore chart for Bryan and i that follows the suggested daily, weekly, and monthly items you listed and ensures an equal division of the work and no more than 30 mins of cleaning for either of us each day

Certainly! Here's a possible chore chart that incorporates the daily, weekly, and monthly cleaning tasks:

|        | Sunday   | Monday    | Tuesday   | Wednesday | Thursday  | Friday    | Saturday |
|--------|----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|----------|
| Daily  | Bryan/You| Bryan/You | Bryan/You | Bryan/You | Bryan/You | Bryan/You | Bryan/You|
| Weekly | You      | Bryan     | You       | Bryan     | You       | Bryan     | You      |
| Monthly| Bryan    | You       | Bryan     | You       | Bryan    | You        | Bryan     |

In this chore chart, each person alternates between tasks on a daily basis to ensure that no one is doing the same task every day. The daily tasks include making beds, wiping down kitchen surfaces, sweeping/vacuuming high traffic areas, and cleaning the bathroom surfaces. 

Weekly tasks are divided such that each person does one of each task every other week. Tasks include cleaning the bathrooms, mopping/vacuuming floors, dusting all surfaces, washing bed linens, cleaning the kitchen sink and appliances, cleaning windows, and emptying trash and recycling.

Monthly tasks are assigned to each person for the entire month and include vacuuming or wiping down furniture, deep cleaning carpets, cleaning the oven/fridge/dishwasher, dusting blinds and baseboards, washing curtains, and cleaning air vents and fans.

By dividing the tasks as described, each person should only have to devote around 30 minutes a day to cleaning while ensuring an equal division of the work. Feel free to adjust the tasks and frequency to suit your needs and schedules.

How can I express to Bryan that I am not feeling appreciated when he asks me to do things like cover the bills but says so in an entitled way that suggests he doesn’t have a responsibility to pay the bills on time and if he believes that I have a savings account this requires me to just cover the bills without complaint even though I do not have a savings account and do not have a lot of extra money right now? And how do I express to him that saying thank you and apologizing to me for the extra financial burden is a must and makes me feel really terrible when he doesn’t do this and makes it seem like I’m being rude for expressing that I don’t want to pay for his bills due to financial strain I’m already facing without upsetting him or causing more problems

1. Acknowledge the issue: Start by acknowledging that you've noticed a pattern of
